abstract: Value – a core concept of business marketing theory and practice – plays a pivotal role for companies’ long-term survival, success and competitive advantage (Lindgreen et al., 2012) and managerial decisions (Corsaro, 2019; Eggert et al., 2019). However, it can only be created when actors interact and exchange their competences and capabilities (Kähkönen and Lintukangas, 2018; Möller and Törrönen, 2003), share trust, knowledge-sharing routines and effective governance (Dyer et al., 2018) as “business relationships provide means for creating and appropriating superior value in the marketplace” (Wagner et al., 2010, p. 840). Thus, value is both the reason and outcome of interactions and business relationships (Corsaro and Snehota, 2010; Nätti et al., 2014), and it is not created in isolation. Instead, it is shaped by individual actors’ interactions with close business partners and actors’ ongoing engagement in interdependent direct and indirect relationships within a wider network setting (Möller and Törrönen, 2003; Munksgaard et al., 2024; La Rocca and Snehota, 2014).Interactions contribute to the creation of value through “combining and recombining resources, coordinating activities and connecting individuals within and across businesses” (Ford et al., 2017, p. 30). It is crucial as resources themselves have no value (La Rocca and Snehota, 2014) and only acquiring, bundling and leveraging heterogeneous resources (Wu and Cavusgil, 2006) leads to value creation (Baraldi and Wagrell, 2022; Ferreira et al., 2025; Prenkert et al., 2022; Tunisini et al., 2023). The notion of resources in value creation is even more important as although research suggests that value creation has a positive impact on performance (La Rocca and Snehota, 2014; Sullivan et al., 2012), others argue that value creation and performance are distinct as value must be weighed against the cost of acquiring resources to assess performance accurately (Grahovac and Miller, 2009). The assessment of value is even more complex as value is a dynamic concept (Eggert et al., 2006), highly dependent on the relationship context and the subjective assessment of those involved in the activities (Ferreira et al., 2025; Kähkönen and Lintukangas, 2018). Therefore, the assessment of value is not straightforward, needing consideration of time, network embeddedness and different phases of the relationships’ development (Möller and Törrönen, 2003) on both monetary outcomes (Lai et al., 2015) and the overall nonmonetary gains and sacrifices (Kähkönen and Lintukangas, 2018).Although all companies aim at creating and capturing value, they face several challenges in this process, linked with the divergent goals of network actors that impact cooperation, challenges with introducing effective governance mechanisms in cooperative arrangements (Dai et al., 2024), differences in business relationships lifecycles (Eggert et al., 2006), interdependencies and tensions (Chou and Zolkiewski, 2018) that impact value process in business relationships (Eggert et al., 2006).Even if many studies have covered the phenomenon of value creation (Chou and Zolkiewski, 2018; Håkansson and Ford, 2002; Munksgaard et al., 2024; Riuttala et al., 2024), the concept of value continues to evolve as organizations navigate increasingly complex and dynamic business networks (Clemens et al., 2025; Fonfara et al., 2018). Change is an inherent feature of business relationships and networks (Håkansson and Snehota, 1995). Ongoing interactions within business relationships unfold over time and are considered a key mechanism through which networks change, allowing firms to adapt to the dynamics of the modern business landscape (Fonfara et al., 2018; Håkansson et al., 2009). When networks are understood as sets of various interconnected relationships (Håkansson and Ford, 2002), then changes within individual relationships, as well as the emergence of new relationships, the dissolution of existing ones and shifts in interlinked actors, resources or activities, collectively contribute to the reconfiguration of the entire network structure (Fonfara et al., 2018; Knoben et al., 2006). Causes of change in business networks can be endogenous (linked to the business relationships) and/or exogenous, brought about by objective phenomena from the network context and business landscape (Fonfara et al., 2018). The rapid changes taking place in the business landscape mean that the processes and bases for creating value change as well. Organizations need to adapt to, among others, global competition and performance pressures, digital transformations and sustainability imperatives.Drawing on diverse empirical settings, theoretical frameworks and methodological approaches, this special issue presents a collection of studies that offer fresh insights into how value is created, co-created and appropriated across varied and changing network configurations. The papers are grouped into two interconnected themes:Increasing internationalization of activities, the search for favorable production locations and cheaper resources on the one hand, and global value chain disruptions and identified deglobalization processes on the other, mean that the context of companies’ activities, relationships and networks is changing (Aaldering and Song, 2021; Brychko et al., 2023), which affects the processes of value creation. Changes in supply chains in an international context, characterized by uncertainty, volatility, complexity and ambiguity, entail the need for reconfiguration and transformations of already existing network relationships (Guercini et al., 2025). New technologies are another source of change in business networks, enhancing organizations’ agility and improving decision-making (Seo et al., 2024). Technological advancement and the growing internationalization of business have undeniably heightened competitive pressures, prompting both large and small companies to seek innovative strategies to safeguard their network positions by cultivating relationships (Fonfara et al., 2018). Moreover, as in the present business reality, all companies “to a varying degree [are] embedded in both domestic and international networks” (Ratajczak-Mrozek, 2017, p. 10), the question arises of the globally embedded nature of competition affecting value creation.In this special issue, Chiao, Huang et al. (2025) examine coopetition dynamics within and across alliances in the global shipping industry. They explore how strategic cooperation or competition with alliance partners influences focal organization performance. Their findings reveal that collaboration with rivals can reduce inter-alliance rivalry but increase internal tensions, both of which negatively affect performance. Their study contributes to strategic alliance literature by incorporating the multilayered structure of strategic alliances and elucidating the nuanced dynamics of coopetition. Their findings suggest that companies must carefully evaluate partner choices to enhance synergy and performance. Furthermore, they emphasize the strategic importance of balancing collaboration and rivalry within complex alliance networks.Coopetition is also part of the analysis conducted by Vieira et al. (2025), who investigate the role of institutions in shaping value creation in a Brazilian wine-producing region with Geographical Indication (GI) status. Based on longitudinal qualitative data, they show how formal and informal institutions foster coopetition among wineries, contributing to the development and legitimacy of the GI. However, the study also reveals risks of value destruction due to a lack of shared understanding around value capture and imbalanced value capture. The authors provide a dynamic view of institutional influence on regional development, value tensions and collective value strategies within locally embedded production systems. The study highlights the complex balance between collective value creation and individual companies’ gains.The interplay between local and global embeddedness and value is also addressed by Chiao, Chang et al. (2025). The authors examine the effects of bribery on the local performance of multinational corporations, focusing on the mediating role of local networks and the moderating role of informal competition. Using large-scale panel data from over 4,600 observations of Taiwanese multinationals, they find that bribery can improve local performance through enhanced network embeddedness in local networks, particularly in environments with high informal competition. The authors also show that informal competition strengthens this indirect effect by shaping the network’s efficacy. They highlight the dual-edged nature of bribery, both beneficial for access and risky due to cost and legal exposure. Their research challenges conventional understandings of bribery by framing it within network theory and institutional contexts. Furthermore, their findings offer a nuanced view of performance in international business, particularly in regions with strong informal institutions and weak enforcement mechanisms.Responding to the increasing interest in the complexities of public procurement in international contexts, Taheriruh et al. (2025) present a case study of public organizations in a developing country that investigates the integration of interaction into standardized public procurement, with a particular focus on relational frictions. The study explores how nonstandardized relational interfaces in traditional procurement of professional services lead to frictions among actors, and how these frictions are managed and distributed. It highlights the misalignment in activity links, disruption in resource ties and tension in actor bonds when changing relational interfaces in a heavily standardized environment of a developing country. By revealing the coping strategies used by actors to navigate these challenges, the paper advances theoretical insights into the nature and dynamics of relational frictions within business relationships. It contributes to the literature on public procurement innovation and value creation by showing how frictions, while often seen as barriers, can also become sites of negotiation and adjustment.The modern competition landscape undergoes severe changes due to digital transformation, which impacts value creation processes (Corsaro and Anzivino, 2021; Falkenreck and Wagner, 2022; Hauke-Lopes et al., 2023). As Holmes et al. (2023) claimed, digitalization processes underpin the existing business networks where value is generated from direct and indirect utilization of data. In that aspect, the distinction between digital and nondigital resources in terms of value processes is proposed (Ferreira et al., 2025; Hauke-Lopes et al., 2023). Digitalization impacts value creation and capture forms due to increasing data accessibility and increased efficiency in data transfer (Ferreira et al., 2025).In this special issue, Shashi et al. (2025) provide a comprehensive literature review to map the current landscape of B2B marketing digitalization. They identify 6 research streams and propose 16 future research directions that span decision-making, digital strategies and performance outcomes. The study introduces digital value creation frameworks that emphasize marketing analytics and decision tools as core to B2B performance. Furthermore, the authors highlight barriers that impede digital transformation and underscore the importance of strategic and tactical adjustments in digital adoption. By synthesizing fragmented research, they offer a structured foundation for advancing both theory and practice in digital transformation within B2B markets.Digitalization of a competitive landscape forms part of the discussion undertaken by Song et al. (2025), who analyze the informal Daigou business model in the luxury sector, emphasizing how social media platforms and Guanxi networks facilitate value creation. Through interviews and observations, the authors break down value creation into prepurchase, purchase and postpurchase stages. They analyze how Daigou sellers use platforms like WeChat to build trust-based relationships, facilitated by Chinese Guanxi networks. Their work proposes a new social media business model that integrates informal practices with digital strategies, revealing new insights into trust-based value co-creation in informal sectors.The up-to-date research increasingly focuses on value from the sustainability perspective (Keränen et al., 2021; Manninen et al., 2024; Sabatini et al., 2021). Sustainability is an important factor in value creation and capture and a source of competitive advantage as sustainable practices can improve both environmental and financial performance (Pajunen et al., 2016). Sustainable business models require participation from interconnected network actors, including, e.g. suppliers, manufacturers and customers (Biggemann et al., 2014). These often involve maintaining, unlocking and sharing value through specific activities that are aimed at using the organization’s business model potential to contribute to sustainable value (Lüdeke-Freund et al., 2024). The challenges companies face in terms of sustainable value are linked with specific industry limitations (Riuttala et al., 2024), policy or policymakers for products (Baraldi and Wagrell, 2022) or integration of sustainability into a new venture (Sabatini et al., 2021). These topics are further developed by several papers in this special issue. Petrucci et al. (2025) identify how the business incubation industry is evolving in response to demand for environmentally sustainable business development. The authors adopt a qualitative interpretivist approach, using interviews with 17 actors from the Emilia Romagna region in Italy. They categorize incubators into “local scouts” and “trendy scouts,” based on the strategic orientation toward sustainability. Local scouts are small and medium-sized incubators embedded in regional ecosystems, lacking formal sustainability strategies, while trendy scouts are larger incubators and investment funds with dedicated strategies for sustainability, proactively investing in sustainability-driven innovation and technology transfer. The different strategic orientations affect the ability to support sustainable new ventures. This study provides a networked view of incubation ecosystems and their role in sustainability transitions, illustrating how sustainability is becoming a central force in shaping incubation strategies.An important element of sustainability is a social value defined as contributing to societal welfare in ways that surpass an organization’s direct interests or regulatory requirements, including voluntary actions that respond to the needs of both present and future generations (de la Cruz Jara and Spanjol, 2025). Addressing social value, in this special issue, Pojiltov and Mainela (2025) examine multiactor value co-creation in a Nordic health-care innovation network, focusing on how organizational goals and societal needs intersect in value co-creation. Through qualitative case analysis, they show how various actors embed both organizational goals and societal “objects” into collaborative innovation. They identify mechanisms termed “bridging-in” and “bridging-out” through which actors align their goals with broader societal needs, thus facilitating sustainable value co-creation. This approach expands the understanding of value creation beyond organization-centric performance and positions value creation within broader societal change, emphasizing the importance of network-level alignment with public health goals.Social value creation is also central to the discussion by Siemieniako et al. (2025), who develop a conceptual framework linking anti-modern slavery practices with power asymmetries and social value creation in business networks. By analyzing ten exemplar practices, the study maps a five-phase process of resource and competence development in addressing modern slavery. The authors highlight the potential of business networks, especially dyadic and multiactor relationships, to become platforms for human rights–oriented transformation. The framework emphasizes the dual role of power asymmetry, both enabling and constraining social value outcomes, within business relationships. The study adds a novel dimension to the understanding of ethical business practices and their embeddedness in network dynamics, offering pathways for further research on social justice in business networks.The provision of sustainable and socially responsible business practices is inherently linked to the idea of the circular economy. In their study, Dziubaniuk and Aarikka-Stenroos (2025) explore ethical value co-creation in the circular economy ecosystem of the Finnish textile industry. Using a qualitative single-case study, they examine interactions among business and institutional actors committed to sustainability. The authors propose a framework fostering collaborative innovation and driving circular economy transitions that link ethical values such as trust, transparency and shared goals with collaborative innovation and sustainability goals. The study emphasizes the role of institutional and business actors in orchestrating value through ethical embeddedness to achieve sustainability outcomes.Similarly, circular economy forms part of research by Andersson Wänström et al. (2025), who evaluate renting models in the clothing industry through a resource interaction lens rooted in the IMP approach. Their multiple case study of Swedish startups identifies three business models: membership, subscription and individual item renting, each deploying different strategies for mobilizing and combining resources to create value. The authors emphasize that while the models align with sustainable goals and consumption, they pose economic challenges due to long-term return on investment. They provide a framework for understanding how renting models can drive circular economy solutions and inform investment strategies requiring longer time horizons.The relational perspective allows “embracing a broader understanding of value” (Munksgaard et al., 2024, p. 929). This special issue expands the understanding of the specifics of value creation processes from both local and international perspectives in rapidly changing business relationships and networks. In addition, it identifies empirically verified successful and sustainable business practices concerning the management of relationships leading to value creation.Collectively, the papers in this special issue showcase how value is shaped through complex interactions among actors, institutions and resources in changing business networks. Whether addressing sustainability imperatives, navigating institutional complexity or leveraging the global context of operation, the studies highlight the importance of relational and contextual dynamics. The diversity of industries, actors and theoretical lenses represented in this issue reflects the multifaceted nature of value and its transformations in increasingly interconnected business networks. The findings collectively underscore that value is not only co-created but also embedded in institutional, cultural and sectoral contexts that shape and constrain interaction patterns.Building on the insights from this issue, we identify several avenues for future research. First, there is a growing need to adopt a more international perspective on value creation and co-creation, particularly in the context of the circular economy and sustainability transitions. Exploring actors’ interactions across different countries can help uncover how values and processes circulate and adapt globally, and how societal needs embedded in national contexts shape business relationships. This is particularly pertinent in settings that differ in terms of economic development, institutional maturity and social priorities. Thus, more research is needed comparing, e.g. cases from developed and developing economies. Furthermore, comparative studies that cut across countries, regions or even just different sectors (e.g. from health tech to fashion or recycling industries) can illuminate how value is understood and created differently, or in some cases, how it flows and transforms across sectors.Second, governance mechanisms and institutional settings are critical in shaping the rules and norms around value creation and appropriation. We encourage further research into the interplay of formal and informal institutions, with particular attention to how these frameworks either support or hinder collective value creation and/or coopetition. Integrated studies examining the coevolution of institutions and business networks will be especially promising.Third, ethical values and values of individual people, e.g. entrepreneurs, remain underexplored yet of value dynamics, especially within small companies and is also a need to the of value co-creation at the of individual actors, especially in complex and ethical orientations of actors, particularly in the context of the circular economy and sustainability, provide insights into both enabling and constraining in network as the landscape of interaction increasingly digital social media and it is to with change and examine how new the dynamics of value co-creation, and even the rapid of these tools methodological and conceptual challenges that future research must while the papers in this special issue the they also highlight the complexities and evolving nature of value in changing business networks. abstract: India seemed to have all the right ingredients for growth: a well-educated population; the legacy of good British institutions (the English language, democracy, a strong legal tradition, bureaucracy, and Asia's oldest stock exchange); and geographical advantages (many ports and being located mid-way between East Asia and Europe). The large population was once considered to be a hindrance to growth, but now it provides the advantage of a large domestic market that would enable the reaping of scale economies. But for so long, India defied its destiny to become one of the growth centers in the world. Maybe this was because it adopted the wrong economic development model (socialistic planning), or maybe this was because of political inattention to the economy. Gradually, socialism was modified in the 1980s and later abandoned at around the same time the Berlin Wall fell. Growth rates since the 1990s have been remarkable. During the last 5 years, the average growth rate has accelerated to 8.5%, and the growth rate of per capita income has been 7% per annum. Is this at last the moment of takeoff of the giant economy into a high-growth orbit that many Asian countries, from Japan to South Korea, to Thailand, to China, have traveled before? What made this economic takeoff possible? What are the potential risks that lie ahead? These are the fundamental questions we had in mind when we started to plan this issue of the Asian Economic Policy Review. India has experienced a gradual increase in gross domestic product (GDP) growth rates. The average growth rate was 3% from the 1960s to 1980s, then it went up to 5% in the 1990s, and 8% after 2003. Many point to the role of reforms in the 1980s and 1990s in bringing about this growth acceleration. Some make a distinction between the two: the pro-business reforms in the 1980s, and the pro-market reforms in the 1990s (see Rodrick & Subramanian, 2005; Kochhar et al., 2006). Some find the process of growth acceleration puzzling. Why did growth not jump up to the 10% level upon the implementation of these reforms just like in China in the 1990s? However, we need to examine whether the reform was of the big bang type or just a series of small reforms, and how much the reforms would trigger a burst of business activity. Panagariya (2008; pp. 16–21) summarizes the debate over the relative roles of the reforms in the 1980s versus the reforms in the beginning of the 1990s. The mainstream view, which is also echoed in all the papers and the conference discussions in this issue, is that the reforms at the beginning of the 1990s are the crucial ones. Many emphasize that the reforms took place in several installments. This explains the gradual increase in the growth rates in the 1990s until recently. However, there are some critics in the literature. DeLong (2003) is skeptical of the defining role of the 1990s reform. He argues that the growth acceleration started in the early 1980s with some reforms, and that the reforms in the 1990s were just the second wave. DeLong (2003; p. 186) states that “in the absence of the second wave of reforms in the 1990s, it is unlikely that the rapid growth of the second half of the 1980s could be sustained. But hard evidence to support such a strong counterfactual judgment is lacking.”Rodrick and Subramanian (2005) suggest that an “attitudinal change” on the part of the government in favor of private businesses around 1980 may have been enough to ignite growth. In essence, a regime shift occurred when the government indicated pro-business attitudes in the 1980s rather than when the pro-market reforms were introduced in the 1990s. Rodrick and Subramanian (2005) argue that productivity surges occurred at around 1980, a decade earlier than is commonly believed, and that the trigger may have been the change in government attitudes toward the private sector. In short, the critics argue that the growth rate was already high toward the end of 1980s, so that reforms in the beginning of the 1990s should have only a limited role in the acceleration of the growth rate. Those who rebutted the critics include Srinivasan (2005) and Panagariya (2008). Both argue that the growth acceleration toward the end of the 1980s was of the type that would not be sustainable. Growth was stretched by fiscal stimulus financed by fiscal deficits. The high growth ended in the balance of payment crisis of 1990 and the crisis was the impetus for the 1991 reforms. They point out that the critics’ claim that growth in the 1990s was no higher than in the 1980s is wrong. It depends on whether or not the 1988–1991 period is included. Panagariya (2008; p. 17) argues that the “superhigh growth of 7.6% per annum during 1988–1991 was preceded by significant reforms, especially in the years 1985–1986 and 1986–1987. But in addition, this growth (as well as that during 1981–1988) was also driven by fiscal expansion and external borrowing that were not sustainable. Unsurprisingly, the surge ended in a macroeconomic crisis in June 1991.” Although the counterfactual of what would have been the outcome if the reforms of the early 1990s were not implemented is not directly testable, all the suggestive evidence in the papers collected in this issue lead us to believe that the reforms in the 1990s were significant and remarkable. However, it takes time for structural reform to bear fruit, and this would explain the gradually increasing growth rate. A separate issue is the role of financial markets for India's economic development, an issue that has been addressed by two recent working papers. display_name: Editors’ Overview

abstract: This edition of Pacific Focus showcases a suite of five scholarly articles that manifest a diverse array of methodologies, merging theoretical discussions with historical case analyses. The first three articles address the incorporation of new demographic groups – namely foreign immigrants, overseas Koreans, and refugees – into South Korean society since the post–Cold War period of the 1990s.1 These articles particularly delineate the influx of these varied immigrant groups, reflecting the shifts within a globalizing South Korean milieu and its adaptive responses to such changes. Traditionally, Korea was a bastion of ethnic uniformity. Yet, this narrative began its evolution in the post–Cold War era as Korea transitioned from a land of emigration to one of immigration. This period saw the introduction of foreigners, overseas Koreans, refugees, and North Korean defectors into the fabric of South Korean society, reshaping its social terrain through their interactions within the nation. These groups have risen to prominence as critical elements for comprehending the contemporary South Korean societal construct and have exerted an expanding influence on Korea's global interactions. Thus, Korean Studies scholarship in the domain of ethnicity, migration, and identity politics has burgeoned in the 21st century, highlighting interest in the internationalization of Korean society, governance by the Korean government of new societal members, and the resultant interplays, perceptions, attitudes, policies, and regulatory frameworks. While the initial three articles concentrate on the governance of foreign immigrants, overseas Koreans, and refugees by the Korean government, the subsequent two articles pivot to explore Overseas Koreans who have departed from Korea and established lives abroad. These articles underscore the significance of their ethnicity, their social and cultural capital, and the networks they form.2 The diaspora's journey to the northeastern territories of China and the Russian Far East, initiated in the 1860s and later evolving into structured labor migration to Hawaii in 1903, has notably impacted Korean societal dynamics. These Overseas Koreans have harnessed their heterogeneous social, economic, and cultural resources to forge networks anchored in their ethnic identities, thereby influencing not only their own communities but also the societies of their resident nations and Korea itself. Collectively, these articles provide an exhaustive overview of the historical trajectories of migration, ethnic identity, networks, and the intricate mosaic of governance and interactions between immigrants and South Korean society. The opening article by Sangtu Ko ignites an intellectual debate on immigration policy governance. Ko introduces the concept of multi-layered governance (MLG), characterized as a paradigm that transcends the traditional confines of government structures, comprising a collaborative framework that includes a multiplicity of actors extending beyond mere government entities. The article contends that immigration policy scholarship has progressively embraced an MLG framework, capturing the intricate interplay between central and local authorities, as well as between governmental and non-governmental agents. This evolution offers critical insights for understanding and refining the execution of immigration policies within various sociopolitical landscapes. Despite the critical role local governments play in the efficacious implementation of immigration policies, the discourse posits that within Korea, their authority remains curtailed, with immigration directives predominantly managed through centralized systems. The article suggests that local governments are tasked primarily with the enactment of directives from higher echelons, necessitating an empowerment that endows them with greater authority and responsibility, particularly when offering direct administrative support to immigrants in their locales. It advocates for a strategic reallocation of responsibilities between central and local governments to substantially enhance the efficacy of immigration policies. The Korean government's commitment is manifest in the Framework Plan on Immigration Policy (2018–2022), which earnestly seeks to establish consultative bodies bridging central and local governments, strengthen cooperation and exchange, and fortify the roles and infrastructure of local administrative bodies. These initiatives herald a transformative shift, repositioning local governments from marginal actors to central participants in the policymaking sphere. This treatise identifies the National Multicultural City Council (NMCC) and the Multicultural Immigrants Plus Center (MIPC) as quintessential case studies for examining the interplay between central and local governments and the dynamics among various local governments. The NMCC is extolled for offering insights into the relationships between local governments, whereas the MIPC stands as a paragon of the synergistic relationship between central and local administrative entities. Launched in 2017, the MIPC is championed as a holistic facility offering a comprehensive suite of administrative services to immigrants, thereby negating the necessity for interfacing with multiple agencies to acquire stay permits, employment documentation, and counseling services. The fiscal model of the MIPC involves a cost-sharing scheme in which local governments provide premises and cover operational costs, while participating ministries bear specific expenses related to personnel, communication, and office materials. Founded in 2019, the NMCC convenes biannually, operating as a platform for collaboration, policy discourse, and collective education among local governments, thus facilitating effective collaboration with central authorities and serving as a pivotal mechanism for the advancement of immigration policies. Ko's assessment of the NMCC and MIPC elucidates the functional aspects of local immigration policy governance in Korea, offering an analytical perspective on the cooperative dynamics prevalent within the nation's immigration policy framework. If the aforementioned article scrutinizes the governance of foreign immigrants in Korean society, Jean Young Lee's article delves into Korea's governance of overseas compatriots within the immigrant community. In an intellectual context marked by a scarcity of theoretical discourse on diaspora governance, Lee employs a theoretical framework derived from an International Organization for Migration report on the role of the diaspora in development to clarify the governance structures pertaining to overseas Koreans. This theoretical exploration is mapped through a progression of five stages: identifying goals and capacities, understanding diasporas, fostering trust, mobilizing stakeholders, and ensuring sustained engagement of the diaspora in development. Lee emphasizes that stakeholder mobilization, cultivated through the initial stages, epitomizes the governance system shaped by the collaboration between the government, diasporas, and civil society. After providing an overview of the status and migration history of overseas Koreans, Lee indicates that Korea's policy focus on this group has intensified as human mobility increased in the post–Cold War era. As Korea evolved from an emigrant to an immigrant-receiving country, the historically homogeneous Korean society was infused with new demographic elements, including overseas Koreans. Initial policies favoring overseas Koreans were predicated on kinship, conferring advantages on those sharing ethnic ties. Yet, Lee points out that the legislative process encountered challenges, notably due to the exclusion of overseas Koreans from China and the former Soviet Union, who had migrated prior to the establishment of the Republic of Korea, within the ambit of the Overseas Koreans Act. This led to significant debate concerning the development of governance frameworks for these overseas compatriots. Lee highlights that prior to the firm establishment of governance for overseas Koreans, their needs and concerns were largely overshadowed by broader immigration governance frameworks. Despite being the most substantial immigrant group within Korean society, overseas Koreans faced a marginalization that persisted until the implementation of a new immigration governance system in 2007, which remained operational until 2023. Lee stresses that the inauguration of the Overseas Koreans Agency and the Basic Act on Overseas Koreans in 2023 heralds a novel approach to governance for overseas Koreans in Korea, one that amalgamates prior fragmented legislative efforts and centers governance on the specific needs of overseas compatriots rather than the wider immigrant populace. Despite these advancements, Lee observes that issues from the 2007 governance framework persist, particularly in the execution of policies. The legal and systemic matrix encompassing overseas nationals, ethnic Koreans with foreign nationality, citizens, and foreigners remains complex and opaque, with legislative processes not adequately reflecting the viewpoints of overseas compatriots. Although jurisdiction over domestic policies for compatriots is nominally under the Overseas Koreans Agency, the enactment of precise policies remains ambiguous. Corroborating Ko's analysis, Lee notes that the central government retains a pivotal role in the governance of overseas compatriots. While Ko's article comprehensively discusses immigrants within a general scope, and Jean Young Lee's discourse is centered on overseas Koreans, Seokwoo Lee's work rigorously examines the legal and institutional frameworks of South Korea as they pertain to a distinct segment of immigrants: refugees. Lee underscores that South Korea, after ratifying the Refugee Convention and its Protocol in 1992, was a pioneer within East Asia by legislating its own refugee law in 2013. Lee observes that despite this landmark achievement, South Korea has been the subject of critical scrutiny due to its notably low rate of refugee acceptance. This critical examination of South Korea's refugee policy escalated when approximately 500 Yemeni nationals sought asylum in Jeju Island via visa-free entry in early 2018. This influx ignited fervent debates among responsible entities, notably the Ministry of Justice and international non-governmental organizations such as the Global Refugee Forum, as well as local civil society and legal practitioners. Consequently, a revised Refugee Act was tabled in 2021, heralding prospective changes in the adjudication of refugee status, with a particular focus on the stages of appeal and reapplication. In an analysis of refugee application statistics within South Korea, Lee illuminates a pervasive concern among nations grappling with refugees: the delicate act of balancing the impetus for offering sanctuary against the necessity for stringent border control and national security measures. Lee meticulously dissects key stipulations of South Korea's Refugee Act, the proposed legislative amendments thereto, and consequential court decisions that bear upon refugee matters. Lee critiques the Refugee Act's limitations by indicating that “[i]n the Immigration Control Act, legislated from a vantage point of immigration control as opposed to refugee protection, the stipulations pertaining to refugees fall short of constituting a robust legal framework that safeguards the human rights of refugees.” Nevertheless, Lee concedes the importance of instituting a foundational standard of refugee protection that aligns with international benchmarks and secures refugee rights. Lee delineates four salient features of the prospective Partial Amendment to the Refugee Act within the government's legislative proposal. These encompass the scrutiny process for eligibility of refugee status reapplicants and the potential exclusion thereof, the annulment of refugee status following voluntary repatriation, the broadening of bases for constraining administrative litigation claims, and the imposition of punitive measures for the submission of spurious refugee applications. Upon a meticulous evaluation of these legislative elements, Lee remarks that they mirror South Korea's reinforced dedication to refining the caliber of governmental decisions in the process of ascertaining refugee status and to bolstering its support for those recognized as refugees, for individuals granted humanitarian stay, and for asylum seekers. Lee's analysis culminates in the affirmation of the essential duty of governments and judicial systems at the national level to provide sanctuary for refugees, which encompasses the development of explicit frameworks for financial and physical safeguards, all the while placing emphasis on the central government's pivotal role within this process. While the initial trio of articles evaluate the governance of immigration, overseas Koreans, and refugees within Korea from a broad analytical perspective, the subsequent two contributions offer an in-depth examination of historical issues concerning overseas Koreans, adopting a more detailed, nuanced viewpoint. Jinsook Bae's article illuminates the entrepreneurial endeavors of a picture bride during the Korean immigration wave to Hawaii that commenced in 1903. Drawing from the autobiography of a renowned picture bride, Yun Hee Chun, Bae discusses the impetuses driving female immigrants and their strategies for survival through employment and matrimony. Additionally, the article surveys the lodging industry in Hawaii, concentrating on capital, labor, and consumer-related issues, and investigates how gender dynamics influenced Chun's initiation and management of the lodging enterprise, facilitated by her integration into the Korean network. Bae posits that from a social capital perspective, Chun adeptly marshaled social capital and networks via Korean women's organizations in Hawaii, accentuating the cultural nuances unique to ethnic communities and the relational patterns intrinsic to economic adaptation. Bae observes that the initial generation of immigrants nurtured members of the Korean diaspora, enabling the establishment of predominant business ventures attuned to the exigencies of each host country. This phenomenon is exemplified by the ethnic network of the Korean diaspora and its entrepreneurial activities in diverse sectors such as Brazil's clothing industry, nail salons in the USA, convenience stores in Canada, and pachinko businesses in Japan. Consequently, Chun's involvement in the lodging business not only mirrored but also contributed to the characteristic traits of the Korean network in Hawaii during that period. While prior research on the entrepreneurial activities of Korean immigrants in the USA has primarily concentrated on the post-1965 influx of Korean Americans, Bae's inquiry is notable for its original exploration of the interactions between human agents and social structures within the nascent Korean migrant community in Hawaii. Bae chronicles the trajectory of Yun Hee Chun's migration to Hawaii, her matrimonial life, her engagement with the Korean community, and the motives and hurdles she faced while venturing into the lodging industry. The article delineates the process of establishing and operating a business as a female immigrant entrenched within Hawaii's diverse ethnic milieu, utilizing ethnic networks and societal perceptions. Through this exploration, Bae emphasizes the entrepreneurial journey of Korean women immigrants and their connection to women's networks, underlining their gender identity and their roles in commerce. As a result, the article posits that picture brides were not mere passive victims of adverse circumstances but rather proactive agents with self-awareness, cultivated through their active engagement in society. Transitioning to Mina Yang's article, we are presented with an exploration of how the ethnicity of a Korean cultural group within the multicultural Soviet society of the 1970s was interlaced with the arts, evolving into an identity and image that achieved international resonance. The Korean diaspora's odyssey to the Russian Far East, which commenced in 1864, reached its 160th anniversary in 2024. Despite the diaspora's having originated during the era of the Russian Empire, the establishment of the Soviet Union saw the forced relocation of approximately 180,000 Koreans to the Central Asian steppes in 1937 under the cloud of espionage suspicions. In the aftermath of World War II, these displaced communities gravitated around newly constituted kolkhozes. Among these, the Politotdel kolkhoz positioned on the Chirchik River near Tashkent, Uzbekistan, emerged as a prosperous Korean collective farm. The leader of this kolkhoz, Hwang Man Guem, acquired fame across the Soviet Union for his wealth, which he channeled into significant investments in arts and cultural activities annually. Yang's analysis sheds light on the ethnic artistic pursuits of the “CheongChun (Youth)” and “Gayageum” ensembles within the Politotdel kolkhoz, using articles from “Lenin Kichi,” an ethnic newspaper circulated by Koreans in the Soviet Union. The “Gayageum” ensemble, heralded as the pioneering Korean performance group in the Soviet Union established in 1969, executed an array of dance performances infused with North Korean traditions alongside the gayageum and other instruments. While “Gayageum” predominantly catered to Korean audiences, Yang demonstrates that “CheongChun (Youth)” achieved renown and performed ubiquitously across the Soviet Union, extending their artistic reach to include international audiences in countries such as North Korea and the USA. Nevertheless, the deterioration of economic conditions in the 1980s led to the merging of these two ensembles. Yang accentuates the enduring importance of these groups in expressing Korean culture and life in Central Asia, despite the transformations that ensued following the collapse of the Soviet Union in 1990. Yang's narrative illuminates the journey of Koreans in the Soviet Union, who were seen as nationals of an adversary state and forcibly relocated from the Far East to Central Asia. Confronted with the threat of identity erosion, they navigated these challenges by expressing their Korean identity through arts and culture during the 1970s, set against the tapestry of affluent kolkhozes that thrived due to their industrious efforts. Within the constraints of a regulated socialist society, the restricted scope of artistic and cultural endeavors not only fostered the reformation of networks among Koreans but also underscored their cultural presence within both the Soviet Union and their motherland, North Korea. This marks a significant point within the diaspora network, highlighting the fortitude and cultural manifestation of the Korean diaspora in a diverse Soviet society. In a broad sense, identity encompasses components such as nationality, ethnicity, religion, gender, and profession, each of which can exert significant influence on how individuals perceive themselves within the framework of their community and society at large. Identity is shaped by a wide range of factors, including culture, upbringing, personal experiences, and the myriad choices one makes throughout life. On the flip side, within the context of immigration, the identity of individuals or groups can be influenced by an even broader spectrum of factors, whether in the host country or the homeland. In the age of globalization, identity politics involves the processes and interactions wherein individual or group identities inform sociopolitical perspectives, priorities, and actions across a spectrum of identity facets such as gender, race, sexual orientation, religion, and nationality. This special issue delves into the governance and identity with immigration in Korean society and the emigration of Koreans It scrutinizes the complex interplay and relationships between immigrants and Korean society, as well as between Korean immigrants and their host while also examining how immigrants their identities within these dynamics. This for more research by and Pacific Focus is to more examining the activities and interactions of Koreans within the global context of Korean society and the wider In Pacific Focus to providing a array of and insights to enhance understanding of Korea's role within the international community.
